name: ATF Warnings OKC
description: The 'ATF Warnings OKC' theory centers on the claim that the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, and Firearms (ATF) had prior knowledge of the Oklahoma City bombing in 1995 and either deliberately allowed it to happen or actively participated in it. Proponents argue that ATF agents received warnings to stay home or take leave on the day of the bombing, suggesting an inside job or a failure to prevent the attack. Evidence cited often includes anecdotal accounts from individuals claiming to be connected to law enforcement, discrepancies in official timelines, and interpretations of witness testimonies. Some versions of the theory claim the bombing was a false flag operation designed to justify increased government power or to discredit militia groups. Mainstream perspectives dismiss these claims as unfounded conspiracy theories. Investigations by the FBI and independent journalists have found no credible evidence to support the notion that the ATF had advance knowledge of the bombing or played any role in its planning or execution. The supposed warnings are often attributed to routine security measures or unrelated events. Critics point to the lack of verifiable sources for the alleged warnings and the tendency of the theory to rely on speculation and misinterpretations of facts. The official narrative attributes the bombing to Timothy McVeigh and Terry Nichols, motivated by anti-government sentiments. The enduring popularity of the ATF warnings theory reflects a deep distrust of government authority and a desire to find alternative explanations for traumatic events.
subtitle: Alleged advance knowledge of OKC bombing

claims.claim: ATF agents received warnings to stay home the day of the bombing.
claims.evidence: Anecdotal accounts from individuals claiming connections to law enforcement; alleged discrepancies in ATF personnel records.
claims.criticism: No verifiable evidence supports widespread warnings; anecdotal accounts are often unsubstantiated; ATF personnel records do not confirm unusual leave patterns.

claims.claim: The ATF was involved in a cover-up to conceal its knowledge or involvement.
claims.evidence: Interpretations of witness testimonies; alleged inconsistencies in the official investigation; perceived lack of transparency from government agencies.
claims.criticism: Official investigations found no evidence of a cover-up; inconsistencies are explained as errors or misinterpretations; government agencies have released extensive documentation on the investigation.

claims.claim: The OKC bombing was a false flag operation orchestrated by the government.
claims.evidence: Anti-government rhetoric; perceived benefits to the government from the bombing (e.g., increased security measures); connections to other conspiracy theories about government control.
claims.criticism: No credible evidence supports government involvement; the bombing was attributed to Timothy McVeigh and Terry Nichols, motivated by anti-government sentiments; the false flag claim lacks a coherent motive and verifiable evidence.

keyFigures.name: David Hoffman
keyFigures.role: Promoter of the theory in his book 'The Oklahoma City Bombing and the Politics of Terror'

keyFigures.name: Charles Key
keyFigures.role: Oklahoma State Representative who investigated the bombing and promoted alternative theories

keyFigures.name: FBI Investigators
keyFigures.role: Led the official investigation and debunked conspiracy theories

mythologyConnections: The idea of a hidden truth being revealed

timeline.event: Emergence and spread of ATF warnings conspiracy theory

sources.author: David Hoffman
sources.work: The Oklahoma City Bombing and the Politics of Terror
sources.title: David Hoffman
